_comment: Fixed set of known public repos run through the RepoRevive pipeline by run.ts. expectedStack/expectedOutcome are documented HYPOTHESES about how the system SHOULD classify each repo; the whole point of the eval is to measure actual vs expected, so a mismatch here is a finding, not a bug in the fixtures. expectedOutcome: 'fixable' (ends up running -> status succeeded), 'unfixable' (reflection should give up early -> status failed_unfixable), 'unsupported' (rejected before install -> status unsupported_stack), 'invalid_manifest' (Part B structural rejection -> status invalid_manifest). expectedStack 'none' means no supported (Node/Python) stack is expected.

fixtures.notes: Minimal Express app; expected to install and run cleanly on the first try (this is the project's own smoke-test repo). Baseline for the happy path.
Slightly larger Express getting-started app with a scripts.start; expected to install and stay alive under the run timeout (counts as running).
Tiny Flask sample with app.py + requirements.txt. `python app.py` imports Flask and exits cleanly (exit 0 = success in the pipeline), so it should count as fixable/runnable.

RealWorld API server that connects to a database/URI at boot which won't exist in the sandbox. No code-only fix can conjure a database, so reflection should conclude it's unfixable and stop early (failed_unfixable) rather than burn all 5 attempts.

Requires an OPENAI_API_KEY that will never be present. Included to check that reflection recognizes a secret/paid-dependency failure as unfixable instead of looping. (If the dev server boots and stays alive without hitting the API, this may instead read as fixable - exactly the kind of thing the eval surfaces.)
A pnpm/workspaces monorepo. Detection should reject it as an unsupported_variant -> unsupported_stack with a specific reason, not attempt an install (from the project's quick-test list).
A Go repo with no package.json/requirements.txt/pyproject.toml. Should be classified 'not_found' -> unsupported_stack, exercising the unsupported-language path.

A repo that exists specifically because its package.json is intentionally malformed (not valid JSON). Exercises the Part B pre-flight validation: should stop with status invalid_manifest BEFORE install and before the AI fix loop is ever given the broken input.
